test_text.title: Shopping for a Meal in Kite Place

audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear two neighbors who live in an apartment block talking about shopping for food.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, shannon.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: How are you settling into your new flat?
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Really well, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: You look like you're going shopping.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Yes, I am.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: My cousins are coming to stay for a couple of days, and I have to cook for them.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Well.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: There are plenty of places to buy food in Kite Place.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: It's the area by the harbor.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Oh, OK, I'll find that on the map.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: What sort of food do you need to get?
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Well neither of them eats meat, but they both like fish.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Well, there's a really good fish market there.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Oh, great.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Where is it exactly?
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: It's at the far end of Kite Place.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: So you have to go over the bridge, and then it's on the right.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: OK, is it open all day?
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: It doesn't close until 4, but I'd recommend going earlier than that.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: It does run out of some things.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Oh, I don't want that to happen.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: As long as you get there by 3:30 you should be fine.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: It's only 11 now, so, plenty of time.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Do you need to buy vegetables too?
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I do, and I want to avoid all the plastic packaging in the supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Well, there's a really nice organic shop there.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Now what's it called?
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: It's the name of a flower.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I know it's rose.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Oh, that's a nice name.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Yeah, it sells vegetables and quite a lot of other stuff.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: And where's that?
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Well, as you reach the market, you'll see a big grey building on your left.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I think it used to be a warehouse.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Anyway, now it's a restaurant upstairs, but the ground floor has 2 shops either side of the entrance.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: And it's the one on the left.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Hmm, that's easy enough.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: You can't miss it.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: There's also a big sign on the pavement, so you can look for that.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: I guess if I need anything else, I'll have to go to the supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Yeah, you should be able to get everything you need.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: But there's a minibus that goes to the supermarket, if you need it.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: It's purple, and the number is 289.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Thanks, that's great.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, what do you need to get at the fish market?
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: The salmon is always very good, and the shellfish.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I'm going to make a curry, I think.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: And I need about 12 prawns for that.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: They'll have plenty of those.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: OK, have you ever tried samphire?
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: No, what's that?
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: It's a type of seaweed.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I just ask for a handful, and you fry it in butter.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: It's delicious.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Oh, I might try that.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: How do you spell it?
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: It's SAMPHIRE.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Great, it's always good to try something different.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I'll see what beans they have in the organic shop, and I think I'll get something for dessert there.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: How about a mango?
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I'm not sure.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: They're not always ripe.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: I'd prefer a melon, it's bigger too.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: The owner also sells a lot of spices there.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: That you can put in a curry, and things like coconut.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Oh, that's very helpful.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: I'll have a look.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: No problem.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I know bread doesn't really go with curry.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: But I always like to have some in case.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: As I said, all the bread is home made, and there's lots of variety.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: I like the brown bread myself.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Hmm, sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: They sell other things there too.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Like cakes?
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Uh, I love chocolate cake.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Well, uh, not that, uh, but they have a whole range of tarts.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: And the best are the strawberry ones.
audioscript.speaker: LEON

audioscript.text: Perfect.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: Hopefully I won't even have to go to the supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: SHANNON

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Children's Book Festival Highlights and Reading Tips

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear the organizer of a children's book festival giving some information about it on a local radio program.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The Children's Book Festival is coming up again soon, and here to tell us all about it is the festival's organizer, Jenny Morgan.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: So, tell us what we can expect this year, Jenny.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Uh, Well, as usual, we've got 5 days of action-packed exciting events for children, with writers coming from all over the country getting involved.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Just to give you an idea of what's on offer in the workshops.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: First of all, there's a very special event called Superheroes.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: This is a chance for deaf children to share their reading experiences with author Madeleine Gordon, who is herself hearing impaired.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: "Just do it" is a practical workshop, led by the well-known illustrator Mark Keane.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: He'll take participants on a magical journey to far away lands.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: With an opportunity for aspiring actors to do some role play.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: 'Count on me' is an inspiring and entertaining look at the issues of friendship for 13-14-year-olds.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: It looks at some of the friendships described in popular books.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: And asks participants to compare these with their own experiences.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Speak up is part of a series of workshops on the subject of mental health.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: This is a creative writing workshop, encouraging children to describe situations where young people experience loneliness.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: A recent survey revealed that children can be lonely, even when they're at home with their families.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: "Jump for joy", as many of you will know, is the heart-warming, best-selling story by Nina Karan, about a young girl's trip to visit her relatives in India.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: It recently received the gold medal at the Waterford Awards.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Nina will get children to celebrate the word joy by writing a poem.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: 'Sticks and stones' is the beautifully illustrated picture book for young readers about a community who organize an African-Caribbean festival to help local children learn about their Jamaican roots.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: This will be a musical event where children will have the chance to play steel drums.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: This is bound to be very popular, so please book as soon as possible.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the program, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thanks, Jenny.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: That all sounds really interesting.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: I'm just wondering if you have a favourite book you could recommend for our readers?
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: It's hard to choose, but "Alive and Kicking" is definitely worth mentioning.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: You won't have heard of the writer, as it's her first book - which is really impressive.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: It's basically the teenage diary of a boy from Somalia who comes to live in the UK.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: It deals with the serious issue of immigration, and all the challenges the boy has to face at school, and with the language barrier, etc.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Usually, books like this are quite sad.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: But this one actually made me cry with laughter.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: On each page there are simple but hilarious black and white stick drawings of the boy with his friends and teachers.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: At the end of each diary entry, there are new English words the boy learns each day, which may help develop some children's vocabulary.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: I think my kids would enjoy that.
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: What about any advice for parents on how to encourage their children to read more?
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: Well, this is something I get asked about a lot.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: There are so many distractions for kids these days that it can be hard to find time for reading.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: One thing I'd say is to make time to sit down with your child and share books with them.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: A lot of parents give up reading aloud to their children as soon as they learn to read independently.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: But this is a mistake.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: It's good to read more advanced books to them, as it helps to develop their vocabulary.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: If you don't have time for this, then let them listen to audiobooks.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Often they'll want to read books they've listened to for themselves.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: I think it's a good idea to make a mental note of the type of books your child is reading.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Often they just read the same genre all the time, which can get a bit boring.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: You can introduce new authors and genres to them.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Librarians should be able to help you with this.
audioscript.speaker: JENNY

audioscript.text: Well, Jenny, I think that's really useful...
audioscript.speaker: PRESENTER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: This is bound to be very popular, so please book as soon as possible.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the program, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Thanks, Jenny.
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: That all sounds really interesting.
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: I'm just wondering if you have a favourite book you could recommend for our readers?
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: It's hard to choose, but "Alive and Kicking" is definitely worth mentioning.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: You won't have heard of the writer, as it's her first book - which is really impressive.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: It's basically the teenage diary of a boy from Somalia who comes to live in the UK.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: It deals with the serious issue of immigration, and all the challenges the boy has to face at school, and with the language barrier, etc.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: Usually, books like this are quite sad.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: But this one actually made me cry with laughter.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: On each page there are simple but hilarious black and white stick drawings of the boy with his friends and teachers.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: At the end of each diary entry, there are new English words the boy learns each day, which may help develop some children's vocabulary.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: I think my kids would enjoy that.
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: What about any advice for parents on how to encourage their children to read more?
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: Well, this is something I get asked about a lot.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: There are so many distractions for kids these days that it can be hard to find time for reading.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: One thing I'd say is to make time to sit down with your child and share books with them.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: A lot of parents give up reading aloud to their children as soon as they learn to read independently.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: But this is a mistake.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: It's good to read more advanced books to them, as it helps to develop their vocabulary.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: If you don't have time for this, then let them listen to audiobooks.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: Often they'll want to read books they've listened to for themselves.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: I think it's a good idea to make a mental note of the type of books your child is reading.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: Often they just read the same genre all the time, which can get a bit boring.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: You can introduce new authors and genres to them.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: Librarians should be able to help you with this.
utterances.speaker: JENNY

utterances.text: Well, Jenny, I think that's really useful...
utterances.speaker: PRESENTER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: What information is given about each of the following festival workshops?
textReadable: What information is given about each of the following festival workshops?

list: will be led by a prize-winning author
is aimed at children with a disability
is aimed at a specific age group
raises awareness of a particular culture
body.title: Festival workshops
Which TWO reasons does the speaker give for recommending Alive and Kicking?
options: It will appeal to both boys and girls.
The author is well known.
It has colourful illustrations.
It is funny
It deals with an important topic.

body.title: Which TWO pieces of advice does the speaker give to parents about reading?
options: Encourage children to write down new vocabulary.
Allow children to listen to audio books.
Get recommendations from librarians.
Give children a choice about what they read.
Only read aloud to children until they can read independently.

test_text.title: Designing a Mouse Diet Experiment in the Context of Science Teaching

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear a trainee science teacher called Clare talking about her practical teaching work to another trainee called Jake.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Jake.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: How are you getting on with the practical teaching?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Oh, it's harder than I expected, but I've got some great classes.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: How about you?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Not brilliant.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: I'm really struggling with my Year 12 science class.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Are they hard to control?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Well, I don't have discipline problems as such.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: It's just that they don't seem to think that science has anything to do with their lives.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: It's depressing.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: They listen to what I say, and I gave them a test last week, and the results weren't too bad, but there's no real engagement.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: And as part of my teaching practice, I have to design an experiment for them to do.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: I was wondering about something on the children's diets, you know, asking them to record what they eat, and maybe linking it to their state of health.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Hmm, let's think.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: So your methodology would involve the children recording what they eat, OK?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But you'd also need to have access to the children's medical records.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: And I don't think people would be happy about that, confidentiality would be an issue.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: If you could get the right data, the conclusions might be significant, but I suspect it's not going to be easy.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Have you thought about doing an experiment using animals?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Wouldn't that be upsetting for the children?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Well, the animals don't have to be harmed in any way.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It could just be an experiment where they're given a certain diet and the effects are observed.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Would I have to get permission to use animals?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Yes, you'd have to submit an outline of the experiment and fill in a form.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It's quite straightforward.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But if we found out that say a particular diet affects the health of animals, the same thing wouldn't necessarily be true for people, would it?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: No, that's true.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But the findings for any experiment are going to be limited, it's inevitable.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: So what animals could I use to investigate the effect of diet?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Mice?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Yes, you'd need experimental mice, ones that have been specially bred for experiments.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: OK, so what will your experiment be investigating exactly?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Well, uh something to do with nutrition.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: So maybe we could look at food supplements, things like extra iron and extra protein, and their impact on health.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Hmm. That might be rather broad, maybe just look at the effects of one supplement like sugar on the health of the mice.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: In fact, maybe the focus could be on whether mice can control their own diet.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: So, what happens when they have access to more sugar that they don't really need?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Do they eat it, or do they decide to leave it?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Then later on, you could do a follow up experiment, adding another variable, like you could give some of the mice the chance to be more active, running on a wheel or something, and the others just sit around and don't do much.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Or I could repeat the experiment, but change the type of food I provided.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Or use mice with a different genetic structure, but I think your idea would be more interesting.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: I might think about that some more.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, can I talk through a possible procedure for the experiment where mice are given a sugar supplement?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Sure, I did a similar experiment in college, actually.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: So, how many mice would I need?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: I'd say about 12, and all young ones, not a mixture of old and young.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: OK, and I'd need two groups of equal sizes, so 6 in each group.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: And how would I tell them apart?
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: I suppose I could put some sort of tag on one group, or just mark them in some way.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: You could use food coloring, that wouldn't hurt them.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Perfect.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Then each group would go into a separate cage, and one group, let's call them group a, would be the control group.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: So they just have ordinary mouse food.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Uh. I suppose you can buy that.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Yes, it comes in dry pellets.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: And the other group would have the same as the first group, but they'd also have the extra sugar.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Would you just give them straight sugar?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It might be better to give them something like cereal with it.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Then you'd need to weigh the mice, I should think once a week, and you'd need an electronic balance.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But we can't hold them on the balance, or it'd affect the reading.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: So you need something called a weighing chamber to stop the mice from running away.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It sounds complicated, but actually you can just use a plastic box with holes in the top.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: OK, so once we've measured the weight gain of each mouse, we can work out the average for each group, as well as the standard deviation.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: And then see where we go from there.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: That sounds cool.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: I think the students will enjoy it.
audioscript.speaker: CLARE

audioscript.text: Yes. One thing...
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: How does Clare feel about the students in her Year 12 science class?
options: worried that they are not making progress
challenged by their poor behaviour in class
frustrated at their lack of interest in the subject

items.title: How does Jake react to Clare's suggestion about an experiment based on children's diet?
options: He is concerned that the results might not be meaningful.
He feels some of the data might be difficult to obtain.
He suspects that the conclusions might be upsetting.

items.title: What problem do they agree may be involved in an experiment involving animals?
options: Any results may not apply to humans.
It may be complicated to get permission.
Students may not be happy about animal experiments.

items.title: What question do they decide the experiment should address?
options: Are mice capable of controlling their food intake?
Does an increase in sugar lead to health problems?
How much do supplements of different kinds affect health?

items.title: Clare might also consider doing another experiment involving
options: other types of food supplement.
different genetic strains of mice.
varying amounts of exercise.

items: Choose mice which are all the same <input>.
Divide the mice into two groups, each with a different <input>.
Put each group in a separate cage.
Feed group A commercial mouse food.
Feed group B the same, but also sugar contained in <input>.
Take measurements using an electronic scale.
Place them in a weighing chamber to prevent <input>.

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear part of an environmental science lecture about microplastics.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In today's lecture, I'm going to be talking about microplastics.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Microplastics are tiny pieces of plastic smaller than 5 millimeters in size.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Recently, there's been a greater awareness that there are large quantities of plastic waste big and small.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The amount of plastic waste in the oceans has received widespread attention, but far less is known about the effects of microplastics in fresh water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And particularly in soil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Microplastics can enter the environment via a number of different sources.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Threads and microfibers detached from synthetic clothing every time they're put in a washing machine, and these find their way into the water system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Other sources include big pieces of plastic waste that are already in the environment, and these break down into microscopic particles over a period of time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On a larger scale, factory waste is another route, as are tyres, which wear down as cars, lorries, and so on, travel along road surfaces.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We already understand some of the impacts of microplastics from studies involving fish and other animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There is evidence that microplastics harm small creatures in a variety of ways.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Such as by damaging their mouths or by impairing their ability to feed, for example, when microplastics get lodged in their digestive system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Surprisingly, perhaps, it is likely that humans consume microplastics.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As these have been detected in a wide range of food and drink products, including bottled water.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as in water that comes direct from the tap.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's more, salt and many kinds of seafood have also been found to contain microplastics.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, it's important to underline that there is not yet conclusive proof that microplastics cause significant harm to people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In many countries, including here in the UK, there is legislation which prevents manufacturers from adding plastic microbeads to shower gels, facial cleansers and toothpaste.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It is very difficult to accurately estimate the total amount of microplastic particles in the soil, as they can be hard to detect.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we do know they are carried in the air.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And deposited in the soil by rain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's more, many of the fertilizers used by both farmers and gardeners contain microplastics.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A team from the Anglia Ruskin University in Cambridge has carried out a study of the effects of microplastics on the digestive tracts of earthworms.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These worms, which live in topsoil, are an essential component of our agricultural system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By feeding on soil, they mix nutrients into it, thereby making it more fertile.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The researchers set out to discover whether the introduction of microplastics into the soil and the subsequent ingestion of these by earthworms would impact soil quality.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And ultimately inhibit plant growth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The short answer was yes, it did.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: After placing three different types of microplastic particles into the soil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They planted perennial ryegrass.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The particles of microplastic, which included biodegradable PLA, and conventional high-density polyethylene, or HDPE, were then ingested by the earthworms in the soil.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The result was that the worms lost weight rapidly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's more, a lower percentage than normal of the ryegrass seeds germinated.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the researchers concluded that this was a direct result of the earthworms being unable to fulfill their normal role in making soil more fertile.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The team also discovered that there was an increase in the amount of acid found in the soil, and this was attributed mainly to the microplastic particles from conventional HDPE plastic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The conclusions of the study make for very interesting reading.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I've included the reference in the notes to give you at the end of this session.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To summarize, the authors proposed the idea that we need to regard soil as we would regard any other process in nature.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This means we should accept the implications of soil being dependent on decaying and dead matter.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Constantly being passed through the bodies of earthworms.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is when soil becomes impoverished by the presence of microplastics, not only ecosystems but also the whole of society are negatively impacted.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That is the end of the listening test.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear part of an environmental science lecture about microplastics.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In today's lecture, I'm going to be talking about microplastics.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Microplastics are tiny pieces of plastic smaller than 5 millimeters in size.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Recently, there's been a greater awareness that there are large quantities of plastic waste big and small.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the environment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The amount of plastic waste in the oceans has received widespread attention, but far less is known about the effects of microplastics in fresh water.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And particularly in soil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Microplastics can enter the environment via a number of different sources.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Threads and microfibers detached from synthetic clothing every time they're put in a washing machine, and these find their way into the water system.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Other sources include big pieces of plastic waste that are already in the environment, and these break down into microscopic particles over a period of time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On a larger scale, factory waste is another route, as are tyres, which wear down as cars, lorries, and so on, travel along road surfaces.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We already understand some of the impacts of microplastics from studies involving fish and other animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There is evidence that microplastics harm small creatures in a variety of ways.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Such as by damaging their mouths or by impairing their ability to feed, for example, when microplastics get lodged in their digestive system.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Surprisingly, perhaps, it is likely that humans consume microplastics.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As these have been detected in a wide range of food and drink products, including bottled water.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as in water that comes direct from the tap.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What's more, salt and many kinds of seafood have also been found to contain microplastics.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, it's important to underline that there is not yet conclusive proof that microplastics cause significant harm to people.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In many countries, including here in the UK, there is legislation which prevents manufacturers from adding plastic microbeads to shower gels, facial cleansers and toothpaste.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It is very difficult to accurately estimate the total amount of microplastic particles in the soil, as they can be hard to detect.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But we do know they are carried in the air.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And deposited in the soil by rain.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What's more, many of the fertilizers used by both farmers and gardeners contain microplastics.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A team from the Anglia Ruskin University in Cambridge has carried out a study of the effects of microplastics on the digestive tracts of earthworms.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These worms, which live in topsoil, are an essential component of our agricultural system.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By feeding on soil, they mix nutrients into it, thereby making it more fertile.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The researchers set out to discover whether the introduction of microplastics into the soil and the subsequent ingestion of these by earthworms would impact soil quality.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And ultimately inhibit plant growth.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The short answer was yes, it did.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: After placing three different types of microplastic particles into the soil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They planted perennial ryegrass.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The particles of microplastic, which included biodegradable PLA, and conventional high-density polyethylene, or HDPE, were then ingested by the earthworms in the soil.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The result was that the worms lost weight rapidly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: What's more, a lower percentage than normal of the ryegrass seeds germinated.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And the researchers concluded that this was a direct result of the earthworms being unable to fulfill their normal role in making soil more fertile.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The team also discovered that there was an increase in the amount of acid found in the soil, and this was attributed mainly to the microplastic particles from conventional HDPE plastic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The conclusions of the study make for very interesting reading.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I've included the reference in the notes to give you at the end of this session.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To summarize, the authors proposed the idea that we need to regard soil as we would regard any other process in nature.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This means we should accept the implications of soil being dependent on decaying and dead matter.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Constantly being passed through the bodies of earthworms.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is when soil becomes impoverished by the presence of microplastics, not only ecosystems but also the whole of society are negatively impacted.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: That is the end of the listening test.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
